metric_name: Survey sample size
notes: Aberdeen desktop virtualization survey conducted May 2011; 76 organizations responded.

metric_name: Large enterprises with desktop virtualization program >2 years
notes: 45% of large enterprises (>$1B revenue) have had desktop virtualization for over 2 years; vs 15% for small (<$50M) enterprises.

metric_name: Large enterprises with desktop virtualization deployed in some part of company
notes: 80% of large companies have already deployed desktop virtualization in at least some part of their company.

metric_name: Small companies planning desktop virtualization this year
notes: 31% of small companies plan to begin desktop virtualization program this year; 23% report no plans.

metric_name: Average percent of desktops virtualized at reporting organizations
notes: Companies with desktop virtualization programs have virtualized 34% of desktops on average; expect 61% when all current programs complete.

metric_name: Companies with >90% desktops virtualized
notes: 10% report >90% desktops virtualized; all are small operations with fewer than 100 employees.

metric_name: Top benefit: reduced desktop support effort
notes: 69% cite reduced desktop support effort as primary benefit; benefits are almost exclusively operational rather than financial.

metric_name: Benefit: improved control of user applications
notes: 57% cite improved control of user applications as a key benefit.

metric_name: Benefit: improved control of user desktops
notes: 57% cite improved control of user desktops.

metric_name: Benefit: increased support for mobile end users
notes: 50% cite increased support for mobile end users.

metric_name: Change in routine maintenance time - virtualized desktops (vs. prior year)
notes: Virtualized desktops show 20.4% reduction in routine maintenance time over last 12 months vs. -1.1% for non-virtualized desktops at same companies.

metric_name: Change in desktop support spending - virtualized desktops
notes: 11.7% cost reduction for virtualized desktops; vs. -0.2% for non-virtualized at same companies. Gap between 20% maintenance savings and 12% cost savings attributed to IT staff redeployment rather than elimination.

metric_name: Desktop downtime incidents per year per knowledge worker - virtualized
notes: Virtualized desktops: 2.9 downtime incidents/year vs. 6.8 for non-virtualized at same companies and 6.2 for companies without virtualization.

metric_name: Change in desktop downtime - virtualized desktops
notes: 23.4% reduction in desktop downtime for virtualized desktops; partially due to 45% of companies using thin/stateless clients with no hard drives.

metric_name: Average time to remediate downtime event - virtualized desktops
notes: Virtualized: 1.0 hour recovery vs. 4.3 hours for non-virtualized at same companies and 3.0 hours for companies without virtualization.

metric_name: Data lost per downtime event - virtualized desktops
notes: Virtualized: 0.9 hours data lost vs. 2.3 hours for non-virtualized at same companies and 2.4 hours for companies without virtualization.

metric_name: Employee satisfaction score - virtualized desktops
notes: Virtualized desktops: 7.2/10 vs. 6.9 for non-virtualized at same companies and 6.0 for companies without virtualization.

metric_name: Best practice: standardized role and user group definitions
notes: 60% of companies with desktop virtualization divide end-user community into role/usage group profiles vs. 11% without virtualization.

metric_name: Best practice: formal IT education plan for desktop technology
notes: 55% have formal IT training plan for implementing desktop technologies vs. 11% without virtualization.

metric_name: Best practice: executive visibility into desktop program
notes: 45% of virtualizing organizations report senior executive visibility into program vs. 19% without virtualization.

metric_name: Best practice: real-time desktop performance monitoring
notes: 40% of virtualizing companies monitor desktop performance in real-time vs. 7% without virtualization.

metric_name: Enabler: thin or stateless clients deployed
notes: 45% of virtualizing companies use thin or stateless clients; power consumption as low as 15W vs. 100-150W for full desktop.

metric_name: Enabler: support for tablet PCs
notes: 40% of virtualizing companies support tablet PCs vs. 0% without virtualization.

metric_name: Enabler: support for employee-owned devices (BYOD)
notes: 39% support employee-owned devices vs. 8% without virtualization; 60% of all respondents currently support only corporately owned devices.

metric_name: ROI nature of desktop virtualization
notes: Aberdeen conclusion: desktop virtualization ROI is more about operational efficiency than financial returns. Key benefits: reduced downtime frequency/duration, faster app deployment, increased user productivity.

metric_name: Recommendation: deploy to most difficult use cases first
metric_value: Expert recommendation against deploying to easiest groups first
notes: Aberdeen advises deploying to most challenging user groups (mobility/high bandwidth/compute intensive) first to identify best-fit solution for the organization.

metric_name: Desktop virtualization adoption: primarily large enterprise
metric_value: Large enterprises primary adopters; small/mid-sized organizations just beginning
notes: Desktop virtualization more so than server or storage virtualization has been adopted mainly by larger organizations; small/mid-market penetration limited.

metric_name: Aberdeen benchmark coverage
notes: Aberdeen self-reported benchmark coverage; research relied on by 2.5M readers in 40+ countries, 90% of Fortune 1000, 93% of Technology 500.
